summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Jonathan Edwards' manifesto calling for a programming language revolution, arguing that software development has stagnated and fundamental change is needed. Edwards contends that programming languages are the key to meaningful progress, and the lack of innovation over 20 years represents both a crisis and an opportunity. The manifesto challenges sacred programming conventions like the prohibition on copy-paste, suggesting that accepted rules are often idealistic rather than practical. It argues that expression-based languages fail because humans aren't stack machines and can't handle deep nesting. Atwood endorses this provocative call to overthrow programming traditions and reinvent the field from scratch with usability as the guiding principle.
key_points: Programming languages are the fundamental technology that must change for real progress in software development
The 20-year stagnation in language innovation represents enormous pent-up potential and opportunity for revolutionaries
Copy-paste programming persists because accepted modularity rules are impractical, not because programmers are incompetent
Human cognitive limits (roughly 2 levels of nesting) explain why scaling up mathematical expressions to large programs fails
Commercial priorities like compatibility and performance have historically derailed progress, as seen with C++
money_quotes: Compared to every other field of design and engineering, programming is an embarrassment and a failure.
Either most programmers are stupid and evil, or the accepted rules are idealistic and impractical. The latter is the case.
This may be the real problem with LISP: people complain about parentheses, but they are really complaining about deep nesting.
We will need to go even further, freeing our minds of traditions established with the very first programming languages.
Programming is at a dead-end. The only way things will get better is with a complete revolution.
key_insight: Programming's stagnation stems from treating impractical ideals as inviolable rules, and meaningful progress requires revolutionary change to languages that acknowledges how programmers actually work rather than how they theoretically should.
